intro: Amazon S3 enters at $5.87 per month against api.video at $12 /month. Both tools position themselves for image/video heavy applications, so the decision rarely comes down to surface features. In practice, Amazon S3 — Object storage service with 11 nines durability, S3 Select, versioning, and lifecycle management. api.video — Video API platform for uploading, encoding, and streaming with player, webhooks, and analytics. Both tools list image/video heavy applications as a headline strength, which is why pricing and lock-in usually decide the call. Both tools surface overlapping when-to-use scenarios, including image/video heavy applications. The honest trade-off: Amazon S3 is not built for small projects, and api.video is not the right call for small projects, so neither is a universal default. Transparency scores land at 4/5 for Amazon S3 and 4/5 for api.video, which reflects how openly each tool documents pricing, limits, and terms. Known gaps: Amazon S3 flags text-only applications, and api.video flags text-only applications in its own summary.
quick_take: Amazon S3 is for image/video heavy applications; api.video overlaps on the same job; decide based on pricing model.
verdict_a: Choose Amazon S3 if image/video heavy applications and the $5.87 per month entry price fits your budget.
verdict_b: Choose api.video if image/video heavy applications and the $12 /month entry price fits your budget.

faq.q: Is Amazon S3 cheaper than api.video?
faq.a: Based on the listed entry prices, yes: Amazon S3 starts at $5.87 per month while api.video starts at $12 /month. Actual monthly cost depends on volume and plan tier, so model your real usage before switching. Enterprise quotes can reshuffle the comparison entirely.

faq.q: Can I migrate from Amazon S3 to api.video?
faq.a: Technically you can move between any two tools in the same category, but effort varies: leaving Amazon S3 is moderate effort at medium lock-in (S3 API became de facto standard; data egress costly), and onboarding to api.video involves medium lock-in (Video assets exportable but player/CDN tied). Plan for data export, re-integration, and a parallel-run window rather than a hard cutover.

faq.q: Which has better developer experience, Amazon S3 or api.video?
faq.a: Both Amazon S3 and api.video score 5/5 on developer experience in our data, so there's no clear winner on that axis alone. The real differentiator usually lands in SDK fit for your language, error handling, and docs depth. A half-day integration spike tells you more than the score.

faq.q: Is api.video a good alternative to Amazon S3?
faq.a: Yes, api.video targets the same job Amazon S3 does — image/video heavy applications — so functionally it slots in as an alternative. Pricing model (subscription from $12 /month vs subscription from $5.87 per month) and lock-in (medium lock-in (Video assets exportable but player/CDN tied) vs medium lock-in (S3 API became de facto standard; data egress costly)) usually decide which is the better fit. Validate on your actual workload. Note: api.video is explicitly not for small projects.
